extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The system under study is a lab‑scale gantry crane exemplar inspired by harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It consists of physical crane hardware that moves small pucks representing containers, driven by a stepper hoist motor controlled by a TMC4671‑Eval motor controller, and interfaced through a Python‑based Physical Crane Interface that connects to the controllers. Supporting software containers include a Python Control Application, a Python Logging Application, and a Python Human‑Machine Interface for operator interaction. The physical system is organised in five container positions and can operate autonomously or together with its digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin provides several services: a Trajectory Generator Service that uses a kinematics model to compute swing‑free optimal movement trajectories; a Simulation Service that emulates the crane and its controllers and can run generated trajectories; a Continuous Validation Service that constantly compares measured trajectories with simulated ones, computes distance metrics, checks against a threshold and alerts operators via the dashboard; a Dashboard (Grafana) for real‑time data visualization; a Logging service that persistently stores crane state in a TimescaleDB database; and an MQTT Broker that mediates signalling among services. These services support monitoring, optimization, prediction, validation and control for both operators and researchers.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation relies on open‑source tools and containerisation: all software components are packaged as Docker containers orchestrated by a single Docker‑Compose command. Core technologies include Python for the Physical Crane Interface, Control, Logging and HMI applications; TimescaleDB as the time‑series database; Grafana for visualization; an MQTT broker for inter‑service communication; and the C4 model for architectural documentation. The digital twin also incorporates CAD models and a kinematic model of the crane, with source code and documentation publicly hosted on GitHub.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The lab‑scale gantry crane digital twin is orchestrated using an ad‑hoc, container‑based architecture described with the C4 model. Six digital‑twin containers are deployed via a single Docker‑Compose command: a TimescaleDB database stores all logged measurements; a Grafana dashboard visualises the data; a Trajectory Generator Service computes optimal crane trajectories using a kinematics model; a Simulation Service provides a physics‑based simulation of the crane and its controllers; a Continuous Validation Service continuously compares executed trajectory measurements with simulated results, calculating distance metrics and triggering alerts when thresholds are exceeded; an MQTT broker supplies lightweight signalling between the services. Each service runs in its own Docker container, enabling isolation, reproducibility and simple resource management. Services communicate primarily through MQTT messages for event signalling and via the shared TimescaleDB for state persistence, while Grafana reads the database for visualisation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The digital twin of the lab‑scale gantry crane comprises a geometric CAD model of the crane, an analytical kinematic model used by the Trajectory Generator Service, and a behavioral simulation model implemented in the Simulation Service. Sensor readings and control commands from the Physical Crane Interface are continuously logged by the Logging Application into a TimescaleDB time‑series database. All stored data are visualised with a Grafana dashboard. Model relationships are as follows: the Trajectory Generator Service consumes the kinematic model to compute optimal swing‑free trajectories; the Simulation Service re‑creates the crane and its controller behaviour using the same kinematic model; the Continuous Validation Service fetches measured trajectory data from the database, runs the Simulation Service on the corresponding planned trajectory, and computes distance metrics to assess conformity. Data exchange between services is mediated by an MQTT broker. All components are containerised and started via a Docker‑compose configuration.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The crane’s motion is driven by a hoist stepper motor that is commanded through a Hoist Motor Controller (TMC4671‑Eval). The controller is accessed by a Python‑based Physical Crane Interface, which in turn is used by the Control Application and the Human‑Machine Interface to issue movement, homing and zero‑ing commands. Actuation is performed remotely over the network via the Python interface; actuation range and safety limits are not detailed in the documents. The system can operate without the digital twin, indicating that the actuator control logic is self‑contained on the physical side.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured by the Trajectory Generator Service which produces optimal trajectories with no residual swing, and by the Simulation Service that emulates the crane’s dynamics. Validation is performed continuously by the Continuous Validation Service, which compares logged sensor measurements of executed trajectories against simulated trajectories, calculates distance metrics, and checks them against a predefined acceptance threshold. If the threshold is exceeded, operators are notified via the Grafana dashboard. This validation loop constitutes the primary quality‑assurance mechanism for the digital twin; explicit uncertainty quantification or error‑correction procedures are not described.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical sensor data (Swing Angle Encoder measuring hoist swing, Anemometer measuring wind speed) is acquired by an Arduino board which is connected to the crane controllers. The Arduino communicates with the Physical Crane Interface – a Python program that connects to the crane’s controllers – which forwards the measurements to the Logging Application. The Logging Application continuously streams the time‑series measurements into a TimescaleDB database. Service components (e.g., Continuous Validation Service) retrieve the stored measurements from the database for comparison with simulated trajectories. Signalling between services (e.g., to trigger validation) is performed via the MQTT broker. The documents specify continuous logging but do not give explicit sampling rates, communication protocols between Arduino and the Python interface, or detailed preprocessing steps.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Control Application, a Python service, consumes optimal trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator Service and issues movement commands to the Physical Crane Interface, which translates them into controller‑level commands for the cart and hoist motors. These commands are sent to the motor controllers (TMC4671‑Eval) that drive the stepper motors. After execution, the crane’s state (e.g., position, swing angle) is measured again by the sensors and fed back through the same logging pipeline. The documents do not detail the exact command format, validation or authentication mechanisms, or acknowledgment messages, but indicate that command flow is through the Python interface and that feedback is captured by the Logging Application.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All digital‑twin services run on a personal computer. The TimescaleDB database, Grafana dashboard, and MQTT broker (Eclipse Mosquitto) are packaged as Docker containers orchestrated by a Docker‑Compose file, creating a local Docker bridge network. Inter‑service communication uses the MQTT protocol for signalling, while data access to the database occurs via standard PostgreSQL SQL queries. No explicit bandwidth, latency, or security (e.g., TLS) requirements are mentioned; reliability is provided by Docker container isolation and the MQTT broker’s messaging capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The exemplar is deployed on‑premise on a single personal computer (edge deployment). Docker containers host the database (TimescaleDB), MQTT broker, and Grafana dashboard, while the remaining Python services run directly on the host after installing the gantrylib module via pip. Storage is provided by the TimescaleDB instance; compute requirements are modest, requiring only a PC capable of running Python and Docker. Scalability is achieved by Docker‑Compose, allowing additional containers to be added if needed. Availability is managed through Docker’s start‑up scripts, but no high‑availability or cloud‑based hosting is described.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The gantry‑crane twin follows a four‑stage lifecycle that is described across the documents: (1) Modeling – CAD models and a kinematic model of the crane are created (Document 4); (2) Implementation – the models are wrapped in Dockerised Python services (Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation, MQTT broker, database and dashboard) as shown in the container diagram and docker‑compose startup (Document 6, Fig 7); (3) Deployment & Operation – a single `docker‑compose` command launches all containers, the Logging Application stores state in a TimescaleDB database and the Grafana dashboard visualises data while the HMI allows the operator to control the crane (Document 6); (4) Evolution & Governance – research extensions such as continuous‑validation methods, fault‑detection techniques and the DarTwin notation are applied to evolve the twin, and the complete artefacts are released on GitHub under an open‑source licence, providing version‑controlled governance (Document 4).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Development uses an open‑source, model‑driven approach: Python applications implement the physical‑crane interface, control, logging and HMI, while the digital‑twin services (Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation) are built on the kinematic model. All components are containerised and orchestrated with Docker‑compose (Document 6). The source code is hosted on GitHub, giving implicit version control (Document 4). Quality assurance is performed by the Continuous Validation Service, which continuously compares measured trajectories with simulated ones and raises alerts when a distance‑metric exceeds a predefined threshold. Fault detection research adds time‑series classification on the logged data (Document 5). Evolution is managed through the DarTwin notation that captures digital‑twin evolution scenarios (Document 4).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin offers several decision‑support capabilities: the Trajectory Generator Service computes optimal movement trajectories that eliminate residual swing; the Simulation Service can predict the outcome of a trajectory before execution; the Continuous Validation Service continuously calculates a distance metric between the executed trajectory and its simulated counterpart and, if the metric exceeds an acceptable threshold, notifies the operator via the Grafana dashboard. The dashboard visualises real‑time traces with confidence bounds (blue and yellow bands) derived from the simulation (Document 5, Fig 8). Operators interact through a Python‑based Human‑Machine Interface to start, home, zero the crane and adjust logging settings, receiving immediate feedback and alerts for corrective action.

# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.

III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

In it are controls for the crane’s movement, homing and zeroing, the real-time status and configurations of the logger w.r.t database writeout. Figure 7 shows that with the single docker compose command the containers and the associated network are up and running. Lastly, fig. 8 shows some traces on the dashboard. Those traces have also been simulated by the simulator, as indicated by the upper (in blue) and lower (in yellow) confidence bounds derived from the simulation. Fig. 8. Grafana Dashboard showing trajectory traces. _A. Usage in Publications_ The first publication using the exemplar is [4], a short abstract on the concept of continuous validation. While the paper itself did not yet feature the gantry crane, the accompanying poster used the crane as illustrative example. Later, in [5], we further explored the concept of continuous validation through the reuse of existing model validation techniques. Here the initial version of the crane exemplar was developed and used in the evaluation of the paper. For more detailled results w.r.t. the continuous validation digital twin we refer the reader to this publication. The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. On the one hand the development process of the crane was used to derive those evolutions, on the other hand we envisioned multiple future evolutions of the crane system to demonstrate the DarTwin notation. The latest use of the exemplar was in [6], where we explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques to find faults in systems in operation. In essence, we extended the continuous validation method of [5] with classification techniques to better pinpoint system faults in the exemplar. _B. Usage in Teaching_ At the University of Antwerp, we organized a Blended Intensive Programme (BIP) on the topic of Model Based Systems Engineering for Digital Twin Systems. Here the exemplar was used as project to work on. We expanded it to an entire harbor system, with a container identification system to intake containers in the harbor, and a ship digital twin with a buoyancy model to teach about correct container loading order. This BIP was organized during the first semester of academic year 2024-2025. In academic year 2025-2026 it will be revisited in the second semester.

I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

is another field for which well described exemplars can be

in the harbor of Antwerp, which move containers between

results and the publications that feature this exemplar. Next,
section V discusses some limitations and future extensions.

Finally section VI concludes the paper.

concepts [10] were originally used in the ARCHES project
on autonomous robotic networks [11], but the authors wanted
to make these results reproducible in a lab-scale setting with
an affordable and straightforward example. The exemplar is
hosted on GitHub [2] and contains documentation on how to get
the exemplar up and running. Emulators are also provided such
that the physical system is not necessarily needed. The exemplar features technologies such as Docker containerization to
encapsulate the examples and is built on the open ARCHES
Digital Twin Framework which is built on top of the ROS
(Robot Operation System) middleware.

GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research

Later, in [5], we further explored the concept of continuous validation through the reuse of existing model validation techniques. Here the initial version of the crane exemplar was developed and used in the evaluation of the paper. For more detailled results w.r.t. the continuous validation digital twin we refer the reader to this publication. The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. On the one hand the development process of the crane was used to derive those evolutions, on the other hand we envisioned multiple future evolutions of the crane system to demonstrate the DarTwin notation. The latest use of the exemplar was in [6], where we explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques to find faults in systems in operation. Usage in Teaching_ At the University of Antwerp, we organized a Blended Intensive Programme (BIP) on the topic of Model Based Systems Engineering for Digital Twin Systems. Here the exemplar was used as project to work on. The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and

_A. Architecture_

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give



an overview of the components powering it. We should note
that for this physical system, we take a liberal interpretation
of what goes in a C4 Model component diagram.
Figure 4 shows this component diagram, again for brevity
and readability only the most necessary relationships are
shown. It contains the **Crane Frame** consisting of all the
hardware constituting the physical structure of the crane.
Two **Motors** and associated **Controllers**, one to control the
cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement. An
**Electromagnet** at the end of the hoist to pick up containers,
a **Swing Angle Encoder** measures the swinging motion of
the hoist, and an **Anemometer** measures the wind speed. An
**Arduino** measures and controls those last three components.
Lastly a **PSU** powers the electronics of the crane.


_B. Implementation_


Regarding the implementation, we wanted to keep the
operation of the system as simple as possible, that is, plug
in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of
the software on that computer. Figure 5 shows the distribution
of the C4 Containers visually.
For the implementation, we bundled all the Python C4
Containers in a Python module named _gantrylib_ . To use
this module, it suffices to install it with _pip_ . This way it
remains easy for researchers to implement additional digital
twin services.

**Dashboard** C4 Containers are containerized with Docker. A

docker compose file is provided to bring the containers up and
down with a single command. For the first time setup, all of
the database and dashboard initialization are also automatically
handled by this docker compose file.

it’s based on PostgreSQL, allowing for commonly known SQL

choice for Grafana was made because of (i) it’s wide support
for data sources and (ii) again the fact that it’s open source.

# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.

I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

is another field for which well described exemplars can be

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.

in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.
